speaker: interviewer
text: The date is December the 3rd, 1996. The survivor is Mr. Victor Dortheimer. His last name at birth is Dortheimer. The interviewer is Simone Redbart. The interview is being conducted in London, in England. And the language is in English. My name is Simone Redbart. Today's date is December the 3rd, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Mr. Victor Dortheimer. And we are in London in England. And the interview's in English. Could you please tell me your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Victor Dortheimer. The first name is Victor. And the second I'm going to spell you. D-O-R-T-H-E-I-M-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Victor?

speaker: interviewee
text: V-I-C-T-O-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before? Wigdor.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you spell--

speaker: interviewee
text: W-I-G-D-O-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is-- did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: 16 September, 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old I am now? 78.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cracow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell me about life before the war started, how you lived in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived-- we lived together, my parents and three brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one is-- The oldest was David. I am the middle one, and the youngest was Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the age difference between you all?

speaker: interviewee
text: The age, two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the oldest to the youngest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And what-- did you live in a house, or did you live in a flat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three room flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they big rooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Middle. It was enough for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you share bedrooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oldest brother, David.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did your younger brother stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Another room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you share it  with anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family just lived in your own flat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any staff?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a garden?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your flat? What street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which street? The street was Cracow 22, Kalwaryjska 70.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Kalwaryjska?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-A-L-W-A-R-Y-J-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the neighbors? Did you have any neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: For example, 80% of our neighbors, they were Polish. Catholic Polish, not Jews. I remember my nearest neighbor was named Kowal, like K-O-W-A-L. You want to know more about my neighbor? They got four children. One of the son was with British army. And he went to-- after the war to Scotland. Unfortunately he died two years ago. And he got one son what living in Dumfries in Scotland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get on well with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: With Polish peop--

speaker: interviewer
text: With your neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're best friends. They're best friends, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get on well with the Polish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends, 80%, my friends, they were Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they Jewish or Catholic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Catholic. Catholic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have many Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I got. But majority was Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you experience any anti-semitism from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in our circle. Generally, there were anti-semites in Poland, but not in our area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went to school, which school did you go to? What's the first school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first school, this was normal school. And the name was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And from what age were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 6 until 10. And after, I went to gymnasium. This was gymnasium [NON-ENGLISH]. And I went five years to gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the schoolS both in Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: All were in Cracow. Everything were in Cracow, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the school that you were in from when you were 6 to 10, what was the school like? What were your teachers like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normal teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very difficult. I've just a few names. One was Balala, and another, I don't remember. But in gymnasium, I remember their name, like Professor Harasek, Professor Krauzow, what was a woman. She teach us German language. Another one, Gurecki, geography. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- at the school, when you were 6 to 10, do you remember the lessons that you got taught? Do you remember who you sat next to? What do you remember about that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make any friends in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friends? We were very friendly. The same-- it was in this school was 80% Polish people, and 20%, maybe 10% Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were in the gymnasium school, do you remember the people that you mixed with there? Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could remember a few, just few.

speaker: interviewer
text: What ones?

speaker: interviewee
text: For example, Bieberstein, Makari, Wengzynowicz, Koscka, Myzczynski. That's just a few. Maybe I-- it is enough for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they mixed schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mixed school, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- do you know what happened to those Jewish people that you've mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember any funny instances when you were in these schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: From '33, when Hitler came to power, there was very heavy propaganda from Germany against the Jews. And they came to Poland, too. And this time I was in gymnasium, and they start to be anti-semite. So I get a lot of trouble. And it was so far that we were fighting together. And they called my father and told him that I should go from gymnasium, because otherwise, they want to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] expel me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father, he would not-- didn't see another way. He took me out from gymnasium, and I started to do be-- because I wanted to go to Palestine, I wanted to be in trade. So I went to learn painting and decorating. What type of-- going back a little bit then, the anti-semitism that you had, what type, what did your friends do to you or say?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they start to beat the Jews on the street and demonstrated against them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where you attacked in school by your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was just once. And because of them, I went out from gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you want to go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I didn't see any future in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you want to go, say, to Germany, or to somewhere else? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany or abroad were impossible. We didn't even-- we didn't even dream to go abroad. Just the purpose to go was one country, was Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents feel then when you were taken, you were told to be removed from the school.? What-- did they foresee any trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is no trouble. My parents are very conservative. And generation, generation they were still in Poland. So they didn't think about just to leave the Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What jobs did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like transport. My father was in transport trade.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Transport trade?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. What was his name, your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dortheimer.

speaker: interviewer
text: His first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hermann.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's?

speaker: interviewee
text: Franciska Freudel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: A housewife. This time, the women didn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And your-- were you a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We been-- my father was very conservative-- how would you-- liberal conservative. So we just-- he went Saturday to the synagogue. Otherwise nothing else. Tradition.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know which--

speaker: interviewee
text: We've been tradition.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know which synagogue he went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I remember, it was-- I don't know how to-- to say in English. But in Hebrew, it's [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you used to go with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes. But I didn't like-- I didn't like to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the younger-- the young generation, it was modern. And they start to go to dancing, and with girls, and smoking cigarettes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you interested in girls at that age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until today. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the synagogue was like inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- the Jewish-English say a shul. You notice differences, are very small, though [NON-ENGLISH] people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a big community?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was big community. But there were-- for me, it was too many-- too many synagogues.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, in your area, there are too many synagogues?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, but too many for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember celebrating any festivals, or, did you keep Shabbat at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my bar mitzvah. That's all. And tradition like Yom Kippur and [NON-ENGLISH] were they keeping.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your bar mitzvah like?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until today, I know exactly the [NON-ENGLISH] what I made. But how many people, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you celebrate it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The celebration was kiddush. As you know what is it-- kiddush. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the relationship like with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like with my mother, I was like friend. And my father was a little bit [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see a lot of him, or was he always working?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw him a lot. But I haven't got to be contact to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your other--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because this time-- this time the people, as father and son, it was something too many, they wanted too many respect.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your other brothers, the relationship that--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were like-- like brothers, oh We always have been like brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you close? Were you close with your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very. Very.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were your brothers close with your parents? Was it the same type of relationship?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same, the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how religious were they? Did they go to shul with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same, like me. This is liberal, everything liberal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your ambition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then? Just to go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was theirs? What were your brothers' ambitions?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same, the same. We've been all three in Jewish Zionist organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- what was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Akiba, or [HEBREW]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you used to do for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Please?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were together almost every evening to learn Hebrew, and and another of Zionists, and [HEBREW]. This was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a social club? Did you have social evenings, or, was it a learning religious club?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not religious, not religious. Just Zionist.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who went there? What type of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Akiba, we've been just young people. But I-- when I was-- I was 18, I went to older people, or the organization [HEBREW]. And we start already to smoke cigars, and dancing, and flirting, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a girlfriend?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eve.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eve. Surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Melnek.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: They killed her in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- when was the last time that you saw her?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? In Cracow, because we should leave Cracow. She went with her parents to a one village outside Cracow, and we went with my parents to another village. So we didn't see each other anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that by choice that you moved, or were you forced to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had been forced to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you forced--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just as people stay in ghetto, or they get permission from the German. And the German pick up these people what was necessary for them to make the arms of the industry. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where you in a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in ghetto after. After, when they concentrate-- this was '42. They concentrate people in Cracow ghetto. And from Cracow ghetto, they sent groups of thousands of thousands of people to gas chamber like Belzec, or Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: If we go back a little bit, in 1933, you mentioned when Hitler came-- became chancellor, that it affected you because you had to leave school. How else did it affect you? What other-- how was-- what other effects were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not answer that question.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Hitler became chancellor--

speaker: interviewee
text: When Hitler came here?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect your life as a Jewish person living in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately. From my friend, Polish, very good friend, we start to be little bit distance. But I blame maybe the Polish church, and the rest German propaganda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why the Polish church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Polish church, they were always anti-semite.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have anything that you weren't allowed to do? Were there any laws that were imposed on you? Were there any curfews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No curfew. In the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: All in 1933.

speaker: interviewee
text: No curfew, no, no. Nothing, nothing. Official, we had the same right, The Jews, the same right like the Polish. But unofficial, they boycott us. For example, if somebody from Jewish people wanted to study medicine, is well not possible. Because they made a law, what is numerus clausus. What they mean, limited-- limited number of Jewish students could study medicine. So most of Jews, they went to Prague, to the Czechoslovakia, and Italy, even to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other Jews stayed behind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you left school, what did you do? When you were forced to leave, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a master what was painting and decorating. And I start to learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of painting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Painting houses, flats.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my first master what I was learning, was Weinreb. I can spell to you Weinreb. W-E-I-B--sorry. W-E-I-N-R-B-E-B.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he-- you were his apprentice?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- yes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember where you first did your painting job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even though I went-- after the-- when I was already skilled, I went to a master, Polish master, was very good to me. And his name was Twardowski. You want to have to spell? T-W-A-D-- no, sorry. T-W-A-R-D-O-W-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was he like?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he respect me. Because until today, I think I was good painter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was Polish. And how long did you work with both of them for?

speaker: interviewee
text: All together as a painter and decorator? The last job was in '39. I was working in another Jewish master. His name was Traub, T-R-A-U-B. And we had been working for a very known optician doctor. And this optician doctor was married, Polish. He was-- had been married with a Jewish woman. Because of that, when that Germans came, they took his flat, what was 10 rooms. And in this flat was a German general. And when the German came, the house, it wasn't ready. So the German force us to carry on until the house is going to be finished. So we-- I was-- I was in this house for the general-- painting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take you to do that job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Another three, four months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you work with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With another friend. With another friend, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because this time they already force us to work. Carry on the David star, you know. So everybody saw I'm Jewish. And it was not very pleasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your brothers doing at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was in pharmacy. And he was working. And his boss, he was a Polish officer what was Jewish. And they-- the German took him as prisoner of war. And my brother was the manager after the German give him another boss. They took away the pharmacy from the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which brother was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: David.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your other brother doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger brother, the same. He learned because he want to go to Palestine. Locksmith. But '38, he wasn't finished with his-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with his learning. So he went to Russia because he don't like-- he didn't like to be with German. In Russia, the people told me, came with the return from Russia, he told me that they met him. And he voluntary went to Polish army in Russia to fight the German. In '41, when the German attacked Russia, he-- the Polish army went against Germany, And he fell. They killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you parents doing at this time, 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, this was not-- the men, they were forced to clean the street, and ordinary work to do without salary. So we're struggling. Just we struggling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in the ghetto there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Before-- until '40, we have been nowhere in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived in the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. '40, we went to the village. And the peasant around, they start to ask what I'm doing. I said, I'm painting. And suddenly it was occurred to me, because I was very busy every day, wanted to-- from Cracow a painter, it was something special for the peasant. So-- but the salary, I didn't take money. Just food. And so we--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you share that with just your parents, or did you share that with other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was living with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zielonki.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is like 10 miles from Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after you were living in the village, how long did you live there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd been from '40 until '41. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That is what-- June.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many months were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Over one year. And who did you live with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a flat. One room. In one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the five of you in one room? Were your-- your brother had left by this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was in Cracow, in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after this village, what happened? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: After this, '41, we got an order from German to leave the village. Because they concentrate the Jews. We didn't know why, but they concentrate the Jews in towns and small towns. So the Jews from the villages, they should go-- for example, to Skawina, what was very small town, they concentrate the Jews in Skawina. And my parents and my relation, what we have been all together in this village, Zielonki, they went to Skawina. And my girlfriend with parents also in in Zielonki. But we didn't want to be split. So we married together. No official, because the official was not allowed. So just according to the Jewish law, [HEBREW]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your girlfriend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helena. Helena Pinkas. So anyway, we went another village, what was not far from Cracow. And we'd been in this village until '42 June.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of this village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wola [POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it a large village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very small. Very small. 90%, 95%, 98% population, they were Polish. But '42, they would concentrate the outside Cracow people to ghetto Cracow. Again, we didn't know why. But when we came to this ghetto, in two days, suddenly SS and gestapo surrounded ghetto. And they took 5,000 or 6,000 people, and they sent them to the wagons. We didn't know why. But after a few weeks, we find that they went to-- this was-- this was village Belzec. And they were built from of the German gas chamber. So we knew that we going to that.
